q: Most fluids are ideal and offer no resistance to motion.
Viscosity measures resistance to flow arising from internal friction between fluid layers.
A fluid with high viscosity flows easily because its molecular make up results in very little friction.
Gases do not exhibit viscosity under ordinary circumstances.
The viscosity of liquids decreases rapidly with an increase in temperature.
The viscosity of gases decreases with an increase in temperature.
Viscosity is an intensive property that does not change with the amount of matter.
The SI unit of viscosity is the pascal second ($Pa \cdot s$).
Dynamic viscosity is measured by a fluid's resistive flow under the weight of gravity.
Newton's law of viscosity states shear stress is directly proportional to the negative velocity gradient.
Shear stress is defined as the shearing force per unit area.
Stokes' law describes the drag force on large cubic objects in a fluid.
Terminal velocity is reached when upward and downward forces on a falling object are in balance.
